items.fix: Commit poetry.lock / Pipfile.lock / pinned requirements.txt to version control.
items.why: Without it, every install re-resolves and reproducibility is lost.
items.example:
```
# .gitignore — BAD
poetry.lock
uv.lock
```
Lockfiles MUST be committed; they're the source of truth for what's installed.

items.fix: Change the manifest and re-run the resolver to regenerate the lock.
items.why: Hand edits desync the lock from the resolver's consistent solution and can drop hashes.
items.example: Tempted to bump `urllib3==2.0.0` to `2.2.1` directly in `poetry.lock`? Don't. Edit `pyproject.toml` (or `requirements.in`) and re-lock; the resolver may need to update other packages to stay consistent.

items.mistake: Treating a loose requirements.txt as a lock.
items.fix: Fully pin all versions (and ideally hashes), or use a proper lock tool.
items.why: Ranges and unpinned transitive deps still allow drift.
items.example:
```
# NOT a lock — transitives still float
httpx>=0.27

# IS a lock — every transitive pinned + hashed
httpx==0.27.2 --hash=sha256:...
anyio==4.4.0  --hash=sha256:...
```

items.mistake: Letting the lock go stale after manifest edits.
items.fix: Re-lock immediately and review the diff.
items.why: A stale lock installs versions that don't match your declared intent.
items.example: PR adds `redis` to `requirements.in` but doesn't update `requirements.lock`. CI with `--require-hashes` fails: 'No matching distribution for redis'. The lock must be regenerated in the same commit.
